name: Centennial Cone
short_description: BLM public rockhounding site near Denver featuring volcanic obsidian and basalt specimens from a cinder cone formation. Free collecting with daily limits on this accessible high-altitude site.
description:
Centennial Cone is a distinctive cinder cone formation in Jefferson County, Colorado, offering rockhounders access to volcanic materials on public BLM land. The site sits at 8,106 feet elevation in the Front Range foothills, roughly 15 miles west of Denver. This is a genuine volcanic feature formed during Quaternary eruptions that created obsidian deposits and dark basaltic materials scattered across the cone's surface.

The cone itself is a relatively young geological feature in Colorado's volcanic timeline. The surrounding landscape reflects the transition zone between the Colorado Front Range mountains and the High Plains. Volcanic activity in this region deposited both light-colored obsidian fragments and dense basalt scree across the terrain, making it an excellent location for finding sharp, glassy volcanic specimens.

Visitors should expect moderate terrain with loose volcanic rock throughout. The collecting area requires careful footing but is accessible to most rockhounders with reasonable fitness. No permit is required - this follows standard Colorado BLM recreational collection rules. The cone's moderate slope and volcanic rock composition mean you'll be scanning exposed surfaces and small erosional washes rather than digging.

This location works well for both beginners learning to identify volcanic glass and experienced collectors seeking quality obsidian. The high elevation means afternoon thunderstorms can develop quickly in summer, but spring and fall offer ideal conditions. Parking is limited to roadside pullouts, so arrive early on weekends.
history: Centennial Cone is a Quaternary-age cinder cone formed during relatively recent volcanic activity in the Front Range volcanic field. The cone's dark basaltic composition and obsidian deposits resulted from effusive and pyroclastic eruptions that occurred thousands of years ago, creating the obsidian nodules and glassy fragments collectors find today.
tips: Search the loose scree on the cone's south and west-facing slopes where erosion has exposed fresh obsidian and basalt fragments. Bring a small loupe or magnifier to examine the glassy quality of obsidian pieces - the best specimens show sharp edges and glassy luster rather than weathered dull surfaces. Look for both clear to slightly smoky obsidian and darker mahogany-colored varieties in the volcanic ash and small rock washes. Wear sturdy boots with ankle support since the terrain is uneven volcanic rock that can roll underfoot.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope volcanic cinder cone at 8,106 feet elevation. Terrain consists of loose volcanic rock, basalt fragments, and obsidian scree scattered across dark soil. Vegetation is sparse high-altitude scrub and short grasses typical of the Colorado Front Range. Walking distance from parking is typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles to productive areas. Footing is uneven and requires careful placement on rolling volcanic rock.
best_season: May - October. The high elevation means snow and ice are possible from November through April. Summer afternoon thunderstorms are common, so plan for early morning collecting. Spring and fall offer the most stable weather and clearest skies.
written_directions: From Denver, take I-70 west toward Idaho Springs. Exit at CO-58 north toward Nederland. Continue north on CO-7 past Nederland. Approximately 15 miles from downtown Denver, watch for BLM access points and parking pullouts near the Centennial Cone area on the right (east) side of the highway. GPS coordinates 39.755422, -105.360747 mark the cone itself. Parking is limited to roadside pullouts along the access road.
road_conditions: Paved highway access to BLM land. Pullout parking areas may be unpaved gravel.
parking_notes: Limited roadside pullouts. High-traffic weekends may find parking difficult. No formal parking lot. Arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Hand collection and small hand tools only. Respect private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No heavy equipment or power tools
Hand tools only
nearest_services: Nederland, Colorado approximately 8 miles north. Limited services - small mountain town with gas station and convenience stores. Fuller services in Boulder, approximately 20 miles east.
accessibility_notes: Relatively accessible for rockhounders of moderate fitness. Uneven volcanic rock terrain requires sturdy footwear and careful footing. No wheelchair accessibility due to loose scree and rolling terrain. Short walking distances from parking make it suitable for families with children.

faq.question: What types of obsidian can you find at Centennial Cone rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Centennial Cone produces both clear to smoky obsidian and mahogany-colored obsidian varieties. Specimens typically display the sharp, glassy edges characteristic of volcanic glass. Fragment sizes range from small slivers to pieces several inches across, though larger intact specimens are less common due to the cone's scree environment.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Centennial Cone in Colorado?
faq.answer: No permit is required for rockhounding at Centennial Cone. It is located on BLM public land where recreational collecting is allowed under Colorado regulations. You only need to follow the 25-pound daily limit and personal-use-only rules.

faq.question: Is Centennial Cone rockhounding site suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Centennial Cone is excellent for beginners. The site is easily accessible from paved roads, requires minimal hiking, and obsidian is straightforward to identify with its distinctive glassy appearance. The moderate terrain and short distances from parking make it ideal for families introducing children to rockhounding.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for obsidian near Denver at Centennial Cone?
faq.answer: May through October is optimal. The high 8,106-foot elevation means snow and ice are present from November through April. Spring and fall offer the most stable weather, while summer is productive but afternoon thunderstorms are common. Plan early-morning collecting trips to avoid afternoon storms.

faq.question: How far is Centennial Cone from Denver and what kind of road access does it have?
faq.answer: Centennial Cone is approximately 15 miles west of downtown Denver via I-70 and CO-7. The site has paved highway access with roadside pullout parking. No special vehicles are required - standard passenger cars can reach parking areas, though the walking terrain involves loose volcanic rock.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding basalt and obsidian at Centennial Cone?
faq.answer: Hand tools only are permitted on BLM land at this site. Bring a small geology hammer, chisel, and hand lens or loupe for examining specimens. Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support are essential due to uneven volcanic rock terrain. Bring plenty of water for the 8,100+ foot elevation and pack a small backpack for specimens.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding site near Denver for obsidian and basalt. No permit needed, 25 lbs daily limit. Accessible volcanic cinder cone with stunning views.
